label: verify-capistrano-taxpayers-assn-v-city-of-san-
system:
You are the citation verifier in a legal-exploration pipeline. You are given one authority as cited by the pipeline's components, plus snippets showing how it was used. Using web search (prefer primary/official sources: leginfo.legislature.ca.gov, courts.ca.gov, law.justia.com, casetext.com, codes.findlaw.com, fppc.ca.gov), determine:
1. Does the authority exist?
2. Is the citation form correct (reporter cite, year, section number)?
3. Does it plausibly stand for what the snippets use it for?

Statuses: VERIFIED (exists, cite essentially right, usage consistent with what you found); MISCHARACTERIZED (exists but does not support the claimed use, or the cite attaches to the wrong case/section); NOT_FOUND (cannot locate after genuine searching); CONTESTED (real sources point in conflicting directions, or scope is genuinely unsettled); UNCLEAR (could not determine within a reasonable search).

You are NOT determining whether the authority is still good law (no Shepardizing) — say what you actually confirmed, no more. Be strict: a close-but-wrong reporter cite or a holding stretched past what sources show is MISCHARACTERIZED, not VERIFIED.
prompt:
## Authority as cited (variants seen in the documents)

- Cal.4th 205 and the reasoning of Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano
- Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano
- Under Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano and its progeny

## How the documents used it

[delphi] …fect (7 of 7).**
Every panelist reasoned that the district's own rate-study language — deriving $0.85 "in part" from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation — maps onto the defect condemned in *Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano*. Characterizations of severity varied within the theme (from "direct doctrinal vulnerability" to "close to a per se violation for the excess increment").

**Theme B — Rebate cross-subsidy as an independent revenue-use/…

[tetlock] …ed on top with conservation/redistributive purposes could be argued to function differently (e.g., closer to a regulatory/incentive charge). Getting this wrong misdirects the entire remaining analysis. |
| **Q2**: Under Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano and its progeny, does a tiered rate component partly justified by documented marginal cost (1.6×) and partly by independent 'conservation-incentive pricing' satisfy §6(b)(3)'s requirement that a fee 'not exceed the prop…

[dialectic] …est of this analysis is read as advocacy rather than denial:

1. **The "not a property-related fee" fallback is weak and should not be the District's lead argument.** Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 and the reasoning of Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 make retail, parcel-delivered, metered water charges the paradigm case for Art. XIII D coverage. San Buenaventura is genuinely distinguishable (wholesale extraction vs. retail delivery) and w…

## Context

The underlying question is a California public-agency hypothetical: q2 — Drought surcharge funding low-income rebates (Prop 218).

Verify this authority per your instructions.

canonical_cite.description: the correct full citation, if determinable

note.description: 1-3 sentences: what was confirmed or refuted, and the correct cite

text: {"status":"VERIFIED","canonical_cite":"Capistrano Taxpayers Assn., Inc. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 (4th Dist.); see also Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205","note":"Both authorities exist and cite forms are correct. Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 held that tiered/inclining water rates must be justified by actual cost-of-service differences at each tier, not merely by usage budgets or conservation goals — this directly supports the documents' use of it to flag the district's \"conservation-incentive pricing\" rationale as the doctrinal defect. Bighorn (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 correctly stands for the proposition that metered, parcel-based water delivery charges are property-related fees subject to Article XIII D, consistent with its use as the \"paradigm case for Art. XIII D coverage.\"","source_urls":["https://caselaw.findlaw.com/court/ca-court-of-appeal/1698183.html","https://www.aalrr.com/newsroom-alerts-4059","https://www.courtlistener.com/opinion/2631763/bighorn-desert-view-water-agency-v-verjil/","https://scocal.stanford.edu/opinion/bighorn-desert-etc-v-verjil-33651"]}

data.canonical_cite: Capistrano Taxpayers Assn., Inc. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 (4th Dist.); see also Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205
data.note: Both authorities exist and cite forms are correct. Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 held that tiered/inclining water rates must be justified by actual cost-of-service differences at each tier, not merely by usage budgets or conservation goals — this directly supports the documents' use of it to flag the district's "conservation-incentive pricing" rationale as the doctrinal defect. Bighorn (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 correctly stands for the proposition that metered, parcel-based water delivery charges are property-related fees subject to Article XIII D, consistent with its use as the "paradigm case for Art. XIII D coverage."
